speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four of an interview with Regina Clipper, May 17, 1995 in New York. Interviewer Juliet Halpern. So you were saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, one thing I also was very lucky, my husband had parents, a mother and a father. [SOBBING] So we had them for a while. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, and my children had grandparents, which is very important. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had an aunt, my cousin Willi's mother. She was funny, she was German. German Jewish, but yeah. But I liked her. And most of all, I liked my tante that was who we stayed in Dusseldorf. She-- her husband, Adolf, died in Israel on a trip. And she came with her daughter to Long Beach. And I had them again. You know, we were-- and she was the best. She was the best. She was, as I said, Christian. And she converted to Orthodox, yet. And she was the best. She was like my own mother, very good to me. She helped me with the children when they were little. And I adored her. She died several years ago. Her daughter lives in Detroit, in West Bloomfield. And we just came back from there. Her granddaughter had a bat mitzah. Now my cousin Willi is older. She's not too well. But, but we are here. And we take care of each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what family exists stays very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. But we work on it, you know. Like with every, every relationship-- with a husband or with children or with relatives or friends, you've got to work at it. It doesn't fall from the sky. You have to put effort into it. And sometimes you feel very blue. You know, start to think about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I look at my grandchildren and my children, what pleasure my parents would have had from them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it wasn't supposed to be. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Life is not too easy, not for anybody. There's always you know, this, that and the other thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little less so for some.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. But I have nothing to complain about, really. I have a very good husband. And my kids are terrific. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my-- the spouses of my children are wonderful. So in a way, I'm lucky with that. And I worked a long time. I just retired last year. I worked two days a week, three days a week. And I always had a nice boss. And I made friends with the customers. We call each other, we go to meetings, to Hadassah. We meet in temple, we invite each other. Years ago, we used to invite a lot for dinner, weekends. Now it's coffee. No, sometimes dinner, too. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] But as we get older, we think more about it, what happened then.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think it's getting older or you think it's like the 50th year now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the 50th-- well, we first realized that's already 50 years. And, and you know, think God almighty, where did the years go? Where did it go?

speaker: interviewee
text: '45 [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were liberated and we started anew again. And it was a difficult start, very hard. You have no, no direction. You don't know what are we going to do. What should we learn? And, and then should we learn this, should we learn this, should we-- and then you have to support yourself. So it's very difficult to go to school and onto college and support yourself with what, if you cannot do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but I did all right. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And what I didn't learn in school, I taught myself. Because I was a good student. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I read a lot. I listen to programs. I you know, listened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as you think of your interview, perhaps being looked at by students--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --By your children, your grandchildren. Is there anything special more that you would want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to save to all the young people that they should [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] think about somebody else once in a while. And I know that being idealistic is not in fashion today, but they should try. They should try and help each other. And if they can't go to college, they should learn a trade, a good trade. Because we just can't be a country with all chiefs and no Indians. They should learn a good trade. You always need a chef, a mechanic, an electrician, a carpenter. You nee-- people still need that. And they should be kind to each other. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if they have an argument, they'll talk of-- they should talk about it. They don't shoot each other. It's a terrible thing, what's going on. You know, sometimes I say to myself, I'm really glad I'm not young anymore. I couldn't cope with that anymore, with the guns and the killing, and the-- and the drugs. It's frightening. And it seems that nobody can do anything about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my grandchildren, I wish them-- and my children-- good health and lots of love. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they should stand up and be counted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You have to stand up for your rights. That doesn't mean you have to hit somebody over the head. But you've got to stand up. That's what my children tell me. I have to stand up for my rights. I tell them I'm a little too old for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe that's what you're doing in telling your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, maybe, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for doing this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you with all my heart. It's a wonderful thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand your husband and your son will be here to join you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll look at some family pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really am very grateful for this. Because it's very important that people should learn something. Because we do not learn-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we repeat mistakes, like they say, you know, from the-- from the, from history we don't learn. We repeat the same mistakes. That's what they say. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken when we first came to Germany, to Dusseldorf. And my father decided that I have to have a picture taken. So I don't know how old I was, I wasn't quite two, I think. No, I don't know, I don't know exactly what year it was taken. But I don't know how I got this picture, too. Somebody gave it to me. Because I had nothing from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my first day in school. It was '29. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was all equip-- we had to wear uniforms, like navy blue with a marine, a sailor collar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have a beret on there, don't I? Yes. And this little shoulder bag is for lunch. And on the back, I have a, a backpack, where the books go in. So this is school Dusseldorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dusseldorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is from a dancing school. It was a ballet school, which was owned by a Italian lady, Mrs. Guerrino. And we, we were very good. We performed in the opera house, if they needed children to dance ballet. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this picture I got from the girl that's standing in the middle that plays the boy. I went in Dusseldorf to a, to a theater, where there once a couple-- was a couple that danced. We went to-- we saw the movie. And then this couple came on stage. And I said, this is Margot. And I went backstage, knocked on the door. She opened the door, and I started to laugh. And she recognized me. And she gave me this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this, Mrs. Clipper?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents and me. I don't know when it was taken. I think 1930. That's the only picture I have from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and my brother, sitting on this lap, and me in the background. That was in Holland, in our living room in Rotterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to explain this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- certificate that I was liberated from Bergen-Belsen, April 15, 1945, by the British.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken-- I needed a photograph, and I wanted when-- before I came to the United States. It was about a week before I left. I wanted to have a picture like this, very you know, spiffy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dressed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dressed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding picture, when we were married. 17th of February, 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Long Beach, in a small hotel. And we made our own wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm. And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a family picture of my in-laws, my husband, my two children, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Andy, his bar mitzvah in November of 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you want to describe this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a family picture, which was taken at a wedding. My son-in-law, Jim, Robin my granddaughter, Aaron my grandson, my daughter Ruby, my husband, me in the middle, my son Andy, and my daughter-in-law Sharon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken at a wedding. My son-in-law-- from left to right, my son-in-law Jim, my husband, me, my son Andy, and my daughter-in-law Sharon. And in front is my granddaughter Robin, my grandson Aaron, and my daughter, Ruby.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You want to describe this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was taken at my cousin Willi's 80th birthday. She's standing in the middle, next to me, in a gold dress. Next to her is another cousin. The rest of the family is our cousins and children of cousins that survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a closer shot of the picture what I said before. The woman in the gold dress, next to me, in the middle, is my cousin Willi, who brought me to America, and with whom I lived until I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to introduce my husband, Fred Clipper. And we were married February 17, 1952. We were in the same concentration camp, but we never met there. We met right here in Long Beach. And we have been living here ever since. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Introduce your son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the person who stands behind me so nice and tall is my son, Andrew J. Clipper. He was born here in Long Beach. He went to school here. And he went to Columbia University. And he's now married and to a very nice young lady, Sharon. And he lives in Millburn, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about this project that your wife has done?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that my wife has done-- it's terrific that she has done such a wonderful project. And it is very important to me. Because it is very important that things like what she went through should be remembered forever. And I would like to have-- I'm very much happy about having the tape for my children and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew quite a few people, children of survivors of the Holocaust. And not all them had parents who shared their experiences, as my parents did. My parents were very free with the memories, even the difficult memories. And I think it's very important for people to be open and honest about sharing their experiences. And I am always appreciative of the fact that my parents did that.
